perturbation.original_text: The Definition of "Confidential Information": The term "Confidential Information" shall mean all information disclosed by one Party to the other Party, whether orally, in written, electronic or other format, and whether disclosed by a Party’s agents, principals, employees or representatives, and whether to the other Party's agent's principals, employees or representatives. “Confidential Information” shall include, without limitation, all ideas improvements, inventions, methodologies, works and other innovations of any kind, authored, conceived, developed, made or reduced to practice by the disclosing Party, whether or not eligible for copyright, patent, trademark, trade secret or other legal protection (including, without limitation, formulas, processes, databases, mechanical and electronic hardware, electronic components, computers and their parts, computer programs and their documentation, encoding techniques, marketing and new product plans, production, processes, advertising, packaging and marketing techniques, marketing plans, product plans, technical plans, business strategies, strategic alliances and partners, financial information, engineering data, methodologies and processes, forecasts, personnel information, customer and prospective customer lists, trade secrets, product design, capabilities, specifications, the identify of potential and actual customers, and suppliers and all documentation, materials and media provided by one Party to the other).
perturbation.changed_text: The Definition of "Confidential Information": The term "Confidential Information" shall mean only that information explicitly marked as confidential in writing at the time of disclosure. Information disclosed orally will not be considered Confidential Information.
perturbation.explanation: The original definition of 'Confidential Information' was broad, encompassing various forms of disclosed information. The modified definition significantly narrows this scope, only including information explicitly marked as confidential in writing. This omission of orally disclosed information creates an in-text contradiction because section 2 still speaks of 'Confidential Information received from the other Party' without restricting forms of communication or disclosure, suggesting the original definition still applies.
perturbation.contradicted_text: Protection of "Confidential Information": In consideration of each Party's disclosure of Confidential Information to the other Party, each Party agrees with respect to the Confidential Information received from the other Party, that it: (a) shall maintain such Confidential Information in the strictest confidence; Pareteum Corporation | 100 Park Ave. | Suite 1600 | New York | NY 10017 | United States Tel. +1 (212) 984.1096 www.Pareteum.com NYSE MKT: TEUM (b) shall not disclose, transfer or otherwise make available any of such Confidential Information to any third party, unless such Confidential Information must be disclosed for the purposes contemplated herein, or under legal compulsion to disclose any such Confidential Information, in which event each Party shall, prior to such disclosure, obtain written consent from the other Party and obtain from the third person a written agreement acknowledging the binding effect of these restrictions regarding the Confidential Information; and (c) shall not directly, indirectly or in concert with any person, use the Confidential Information for any purpose other than evaluating the prospective business relationship with the other Party in accordance with the introduction . Each Party shall take reasonable measures to protect the Confidential Information of the other Party. Those measures shall not be less than the measures taken to protect the receiving Party’s own confidential information. Confidential Information of the other Party may be provided to a Party’s employees only on a need-to-know basis, and prior to such provision, the Party will notify each employee to whom such disclosure is made that such Confidential Information is received in confidence and shall be kept in confidence by such employee.
perturbation.justification: The original text defines 'Confidential Information' broadly, including orally disclosed information. The changed text restricts the definition to only information explicitly marked as confidential in writing, excluding orally disclosed information. The contradicted text, 'Protection of Confidential Information,' refers to 'Confidential Information received from the other Party' without specifying the form of disclosure, creating a contradiction. The changed text's exclusion of orally disclosed information directly conflicts with the contradicted text's implication that all Confidential Information, regardless of form, is protected.

perturbation.original_text: Each Party shall take reasonable measures to protect the Confidential Information of the other Party. Those measures shall not be less than the measures taken to protect the receiving Party’s own confidential information.
perturbation.changed_text: Each Party shall take reasonable measures to protect the Confidential Information of the other Party. Those measures shall not be less than commercially reasonable measures.
perturbation.explanation: The original clause mandates protection measures 'not less than the measures taken to protect the receiving Party’s own confidential information.' The change to 'commercially reasonable measures' weakens the standard of care. If a Party has poor internal security, the new clause would allow similarly lax protection of the other Party's information. This is an in-text contradiction with section 4 which highlights that Confidential Information includes valuable trade secrets, thus expecting a higher standard than 'commercially reasonable'.
perturbation.contradicted_text: No Licenses or Warranties: Each Party’s Confidential Information and all rights thereto shall remain such Party’s sole property. Each Party recognizes that the disclosure of Confidential Information by the disclosing Party shall not be construed as granting any rights, by license or otherwise, concerning any Confidential Information, except as may be explicitly created by this Agreement. Each Party acknowledges that the other Party’s Confidential Information includes valuable trade secrets. Neither Party has any obligation to disclose Confidential Information to the other Party. NEITHER PARTY MAKES ANY REPRESENTATION OR WARRANTY AS TO THE ACCURACY, COMPLETENESS, CONDITION, FITNESS and MERCHANTABILITY, OR PERFORMANCE OF ITS CONFIDENTIAL INFORMATION.
perturbation.justification: The original text mandated protection measures 'not less than the measures taken to protect the receiving Party’s own confidential information,' which could be a very high standard. The change to 'commercially reasonable measures' lowers the standard of care and is inconsistent with the Contradicted Text, which states that 'the other Party’s Confidential Information includes valuable trade secrets.' Protecting trade secrets usually necessitates a higher standard than 'commercially reasonable measures,' creating a strong contradiction. The change directly clashes with the expectation of a higher standard of care for valuable trade secrets implied in the contradicted text, therefore the answer is YES. This follows the Omissions - In Text Contradiction definition.

perturbation.original_text: In addition to the foregoing and without limitation thereof, the disclosing Party shall cooperate in prosecuting any claims against third parties for unauthorized use of any Confidential Information.
perturbation.changed_text: The disclosing Party may, at its sole discretion, cooperate in prosecuting any claims against third parties for unauthorized use of any Confidential Information.
perturbation.explanation: The original clause imposes a mandatory obligation on the disclosing party to cooperate in prosecuting claims. The modified clause makes cooperation discretionary ('may, at its sole discretion'). This creates an in-text contradiction because the beginning of the section indicates that the disclosing party 'shall save, defend, indemnify and hold the non-disclosing Party harmless from and against any and all liability and damages suffered by the non-disclosing Party arising therefrom'. The discretionary cooperation undermines the indemnification promise.
perturbation.contradicted_text: Remedies: If either Party causes a disclosure of the other Party’s Confidential Information in breach of the terms of this Agreement, the disclosing Party shall immediately report in writing the disclosure to the other Party and shall save, defend, indemnify and hold the non-disclosing Party harmless from and against any and all liability and damages suffered by the non-disclosing Party arising therefrom. In addition to the foregoing and without limitation thereof, the disclosing Party may, at its sole discretion, cooperate in prosecuting any claims against third parties for unauthorized use of any Confidential Information. Each Party acknowledges that unauthorized disclosure, use or disposition, whether actual or threatened, of any Confidential Information shall cause irreparable harm, loss of business and significant injury to the disclosing Party, the scope of which would be difficult to ascertain. Each Party agrees, therefore, that the disclosing Party has the right to obtain an immediate injunction against any breach, threatened breach or attempted breach of this Agreement, in addition to any other remedies that may be available at law, including without limitation, the recovery of expenses, costs and attorney’s fees arising out of such breach, threatened breach or attempted breach.
perturbation.justification: The original text mandates cooperation in prosecuting claims, while the changed text makes it discretionary. This directly contradicts the 'save, defend, indemnify and hold harmless' obligation in the contradicted text because discretionary cooperation undermines the indemnification promise. The changed text weakens the original agreement, creating a clear in-text contradiction because the disclosing party might choose not to cooperate, leaving the non-disclosing party without the promised defense and indemnity.

perturbation.original_text: All Confidential Information shall be returned to the disclosing Parties promptly upon written request or, at the election of the disclosing Party, the Party that received the Confidential Information shall certify said information has been destroyed and is no longer useable in any format.
perturbation.changed_text: All Confidential Information shall be returned to the disclosing Parties promptly upon written request.
perturbation.explanation: The original clause allowed for the *option* of certifying destruction of Confidential Information. Removing this option and making return mandatory conflicts with the earlier section 5 'Remedies' where it is acknowledged that unauthorized disclosure, use or disposition of Confidential Information may cause irreparable harm. If the information has already been used or disclosed by the receiving party, then forcing the receiving party to physically return this information does not undue the damage of the disclosure.
perturbation.contradicted_text: Remedies: If either Party causes a disclosure of the other Party’s Confidential Information in breach of the terms of this Agreement, the disclosing Party shall immediately report in writing the disclosure to the other Party and shall save, defend, indemnify and hold the non-disclosing Party harmless from and against any and all liability and damages suffered by the non-disclosing Party arising therefrom. In addition to the foregoing and without limitation thereof, the disclosing Party may, at its sole discretion, cooperate in prosecuting any claims against third parties for unauthorized use of any Confidential Information. Each Party acknowledges that unauthorized disclosure, use or disposition, whether actual or threatened, of any Confidential Information shall cause irreparable harm, loss of business and significant injury to the disclosing Party, the scope of which would be difficult to ascertain. Each Party agrees, therefore, that the disclosing Party has the right to obtain an immediate injunction against any breach, threatened breach or attempted breach of this Agreement, in addition to any other remedies that may be available at law, including without limitation, the recovery of expenses, costs and attorney’s fees arising out of such breach, threatened breach or attempted breach.
perturbation.justification: The changed text removes the option to certify destruction of confidential information, mandating its return. This creates a contradiction with the 'Remedies' section, which acknowledges that unauthorized use or disclosure can cause irreparable harm. Returning the information doesn't undo the damage of prior unauthorized use, making the mandatory return requirement potentially ineffective and inconsistent with the remedies outlined for breaches related to Confidential Information. The change alters the possible actions from what was previously allowed, creating direct conflict with the contradicted text. This aligns with the definition of 'Omissions – In Text Contradiction'.

perturbation.original_text: Each Party’s obligations with respect to the Confidential Information, including but not limited to, sections 2,4,5,6 shall survive the termination of this Agreement and/or return of all Confidential Information from the latter date of either termination or return of such information.
perturbation.changed_text: Each Party’s obligations with respect to the Confidential Information, including but not limited to, sections 2,4,5,6 shall survive for 1 year after the termination of this Agreement.
perturbation.explanation: The original clause states that obligations related to Confidential Information survive termination *indefinitely*. The modification limits the survival to 1 year. This conflicts with section 5 'Remedies', because if someone breaches this agreement 2 years after it was terminated, then the injured party has no legal recourse since the obligations have ceased and is directly in conflict of seeking remedies. Therefore, the right to obtain an immediate injunction against any breach, threatened breach or attempted breach of this Agreement
perturbation.contradicted_text: Remedies: If either Party causes a disclosure of the other Party’s Confidential Information in breach of the terms of this Agreement, the disclosing Party shall immediately report in writing the disclosure to the other Party and shall save, defend, indemnify and hold the non-disclosing Party harmless from and against any and all liability and damages suffered by the non-disclosing Party arising therefrom. In addition to the foregoing and without limitation thereof, the disclosing Party may, at its sole discretion, cooperate in prosecuting any claims against third parties for unauthorized use of any Confidential Information. Each Party acknowledges that unauthorized disclosure, use or disposition, whether actual or threatened, of any Confidential Information shall cause irreparable harm, loss of business and significant injury to the disclosing Party, the scope of which would be difficult to ascertain. Each Party agrees, therefore, that the disclosing Party has the right to obtain an immediate injunction against any breach, threatened breach or attempted breach of this Agreement, in addition to any other remedies that may be available at law, including without limitation, the recovery of expenses, costs and attorney’s fees arising out of such breach, threatened breach or attempted breach.
perturbation.justification: The original text allowed the obligations related to Confidential Information to survive termination indefinitely. The changed text limits this survival to 1 year. This directly contradicts the 'Remedies' section, because it removes the ability to seek remedies for breaches that occur after the 1-year period, creating a clear and significant contradiction.

perturbation.original_text: iPass understands, acknowledges and agrees, AS A MATERIAL INDUCEMENT FOR TEUM TO MAKE AND ENTER INTO THIS AGREEMENT, that with respect to any customer or prospective customer opportunities that are identified by TEUM (collectively, the “ TEUM Leads ”) to iPass, that iPass shall not negotiate directly or indirectly solicit or otherwise attempt to cause the TEUM Leads enter into any form of agreement with iPass without the consent of TEUM, which consent may be given or withheld in TEUM’s sole discretion. In addition to the foregoing, and without limitation thereof, iPass shall not circumvent or otherwise engage in any form of direct or indirect communications with any TEUM Leads without the prior written approval of TEUM, which approval may be given or withheld in TEUM’s sole discretion.
perturbation.changed_text: iPass understands, acknowledges and agrees that with respect to any customer or prospective customer opportunities that are identified by TEUM (collectively, the “ TEUM Leads ”) to iPass, that iPass shall not negotiate directly or indirectly solicit or otherwise attempt to cause the TEUM Leads enter into any form of agreement with iPass without reasonable consideration to TEUM, which consent may be given or withheld.
perturbation.explanation: The original clause strictly prohibits iPass from engaging TEUM Leads without TEUM's consent, stating this is a 'MATERIAL INDUCEMENT'. The modified clause removes 'AS A MATERIAL INDUCEMENT' and adds 'reasonable consideration to TEUM'. This introduces a degree of ambiguity and possible in-text contradiction: The original contract was designed with the concept of the TEUM leads, as a material consideration to TEUM, so the removal creates a material change that can impact the rest of the contract. Changing the wording to 'reasonable consideration' suggests that iPass can act independently, but with consideration, which conflicts with the overall intent to have TEUM's specific consent.
perturbation.contradicted_text: Entire Agreement; Amendment: This Agreement contains the entire understandings between and among the Parties and supersedes any prior understanding and agreements among them respecting the subject matter hereof. No amendment to this Agreement shall be valid unless set forth in writing and signed by both Parties.
perturbation.justification: The original text explicitly states iPass shall not engage TEUM Leads without TEUM's consent, emphasizing this as a 'MATERIAL INDUCEMENT'. The changed text replaces this strict condition with 'reasonable consideration to TEUM'. This introduces ambiguity as 'reasonable consideration' is subjective and could imply iPass can act independently without explicit consent. This contradicts the 'Entire Agreement' clause, as the material change in the iPass obligations to TEUM regarding TEUM Leads represents a material change to the agreement, but there is no written and signed amendment to the agreement. The original contract clearly states that iPass cannot engage TEUM leads without the express consent of TEUM, which is then voided by the changed text to read iPass can with reasonable consideration to TEUM.
